linkedin:
Most dental principals I speak with track one number above all others: chair utilisation. Whether the chairs are busy.

That is a useful metric. But it answers the wrong question.

The more revealing number is recall effectiveness. The percentage of existing patients who return within the clinically recommended timeframe. For a practice with an established patient base, even a moderate gap in that number leaves a significant pool of patients the practice already acquired, treated, and then quietly lost.

A full schedule can mask this problem. New patients compensate for the attrition, the book stays full, and the financial drag is invisible until someone looks at it directly.

Recall effectiveness and treatment acceptance rate are the two numbers that define the financial engine of a dental practice. Recall brings patients back. Treatment acceptance determines what happens when they arrive. Most practice management systems contain both metrics. Few practices review them together regularly.

With EOFY approaching, this is the moment to look beyond the compliance work and ask the commercial questions. How did recall effectiveness move year on year? Are fee schedules still aligned with costs after recent wage and supply increases? Which appointment types and payor streams produced the strongest margins?

These are not accounting questions. Your bookkeeper and accountant handle the compliance layer exactly as they should. These are the questions that sit above it, and they do not get answered unless someone is specifically looking for them.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

facebook:
End of financial year is just around the corner. For most dental practices, that usually means one thing: the paperwork goes to the accountant.

But there is a different kind of review worth running alongside the compliance work. How did your recall effectiveness track this year? What was your treatment acceptance rate? Are your fee schedules still keeping up with what it actually costs to run the practice?

These are the questions that separate practices that feel busy and profitable from those that simply feel busy.

If you manage or own a dental practice, what is the one financial metric you wish you tracked more consistently? Share it below.
